The surgeon uses forceps to retract the gallbladder fundus, separating the body of the gallbladder from surrounding tissues. The duodenum is visible hanging down, with adhesions between the duodenum and Hartman's pouch being carefully dissected. Pericholecystatic fluid is present, aiding the dissection process.

The surgeon encounters thickened adhesions over time, obscuring the view of the common bile duct and Hartman's pouch. The duodenum is gently pushed down while fibrinous adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding structures are carefully separated. The surgeon performs lateral dissection to clarify the anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the vital structures, including the common bile duct, hepatic artery, and portal vein, near the duodenum as they enter the liver. The gallbladder is being separated from the cystic duct, cystic artery, and bile duct, with attention to the potential presence of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct and notes the high position of the gallbladder. The cystic duct and cystic artery are considered, but strong pulsations suggest the presence of the hepatic artery instead. The surgeon carefully separates regions around the foramen of Winslow, also known as the epiploic foramen.

The surgeon carefully separates tissue between the liver and gallbladder, avoiding the area below where the portal vein is located. The dissection reveals a frozen Calot's triangle, indicating dense adhesions.

The surgeon cautiously navigates the surgical field with limited visibility between the common bile duct, common hepatic duct, and cystic duct. The dissection proceeds above these structures, focusing on separating the gallbladder from the surrounding ducts.

The surgeon cautiously dissects the inflamed and necrotic gallbladder, with pus visibly pouring out as the gallbladder is mobilized away from the liver fossa. The dissection is performed laterally to avoid vital ducts entering the liver, aiming to create a cleavage between the gallbladder and liver tissue.

The surgeon cautiously navigates around the gallbladder, which is inflamed and filled with pus, causing some pus and blood to pour out during dissection. The cystic artery is identified in an abnormal position, while strong pulsations from the hepatic artery are visible on the right side.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is in an abnormal position distal to the cystic duct, and applies a clip using a clip applicator. The artery is positioned towards the common bile duct rather than the common hepatic duct. After securing the artery with a second clip, the surgeon prepares to cut it above the clip.

The surgeon cuts the cystic artery without applying a distal clip, using cauterization if bleeding occurs. A retractor is used to pull the gallbladder, revealing the fossa on the left side of the gallbladder anteriorly.

The surgeon gently navigates through thick adhesions around the gallbladder, which has been symptomatic for six months. The gallbladder is carefully separated from the liver, revealing the vertical structures below likely containing the common bile duct and common hepatic duct.

The surgeon performs dissection above the gallbladder, encountering thick adhesions and a notably thick cystic duct. Using a tooth grasper, typically for the fundus, the surgeon attempts to hold the gallbladder at the Hartmann's pouch due to the slippery and thick tissue, exercising caution to avoid excessive force.

The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to apply pressure and open the plane between the gallbladder and the liver, aiming to separate these structures above the common hepatic duct. Progress is visible, though slow, as the dissection continues carefully.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the adhesions between the gallbladder and the liver, progressively finding and separating the tissue planes that were difficult to manipulate with blunt forceps.

The surgeon uses scissors for sharp dissection to build a plane between the liver and the gallbladder, which is adhered to the liver. A stone from the gallbladder is visible, and pus is observed coming from an opened section of the gallbladder. The surgeon holds the posterior wall of the gallbladder, pulling it towards the right side of the patient, and successfully creates a plane beyond the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses tooth forceps to grasp a portion of the gallbladder wall that remains adhered to the liver. With scissors, the surgeon performs sharp dissection, aided by suction to clear blood and pus, revealing a thickened vein or arterial branch. The round fundus of the gallbladder is successfully separated from the liver.
The surgeon performs a fundal-first dissection, separating the gallbladder from the liver. The gallbladder is progressively mobilized, revealing the thick and white cystic duct at the bottom.

The surgeon carefully dissects the fibrous tissue surrounding the gallbladder, ensuring not to cut the common hepatic duct. The stone previously removed from the abdominal cavity is not visible in this edited clip. The surgeon gently pushes aside the tissue, confirming it is not the duct, before incising it to proceed with the dissection.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the bottom of the gallbladder, which is attached to the common bile duct, revealing a thickened cystic duct due to inflammation. Using scissors, the surgeon identifies the common bile duct entering the liver and cautiously dissects the fascia surrounding the area to expose the duct for potential ligation.

The surgeon carefully detaches fibro-fatty tissue near the common bile duct (CBD) and common hepatic duct, aiming to locate the cystic duct. The lower part of the thick gallbladder or cystic duct is visible and under control, with no bleeding observed from the dissected fibrous tissue.
The surgeon prepares to ligate the thickened cystic duct using intracorporeal ligation with a Mars silk suture, number one, ensuring careful separation of surrounding tissues to avoid harm.

The surgeon uses their middle finger to guide a thread beyond the gallbladder, positioning it to ligate the wide cystic duct or sessile portion of the gallbladder. The thread is pulled inside to ensure proper ligation around the targeted area.

The surgeon prepares to perform intracorporeal ligation on the cystic duct, changing forceps to achieve a better grasp on the duct. The ligation process is underway, requiring precise manipulation of the duct without the use of clips.
The surgeon carefully pulls the gallbladder downwards to position it for ligation, ensuring the thick cystic duct or sessile gallbladder is securely encircled to prevent leakage upon cutting.
